title: What is a pointer
instruction: Imagine a library. Every book has a shelf location — a call number like 005.133.GO. A pointer is that call number written on a slip of paper.\nIn Go: `&x` gives you the address of `x` (the call-number slip). `*p` reads the value at the address (retrieves the book from the shelf).\nRun this. Printing `p` gives a memory address. Printing `*p` gives the actual value — the book itself.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	x := 42
	p := &x
	fmt.Println("Value:", x)
	fmt.Println("Address:", p)
	fmt.Println("Value via pointer:", *p)
}
successMessage: The address will look different on every run — memory locations change like books being reshelved. But *p always gives 42. The pointer remembers where to find it.

title: Declare a pointer
instruction: The type of a pointer to an `int` is `*int`. A pointer to a `string` is `*string`.\nDeclare a variable `copies` with value `3`. Declare a pointer `cp` that points to it. Print both the address and the value via the pointer.

codeChecks.message: Declare: copies := 3
Declare the pointer: cp := &copies
Print the value via pointer: fmt.Println(*cp)
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: cp holds a shelf location, *cp holds the book at that location. Two different things — same data.

title: aha — change a value through a pointer
instruction: If you have a pointer `p` to a variable, you can change the variable's value by writing to `*p`.\n`*p = 99` — sets the value at that address to 99.\nRun this and notice that changing `*p` also changes `x` — because they point to the same place.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	x := 10
	p := &x

	fmt.Println("Before:", x)
	*p = 99
	fmt.Println("After:", x)
}
successMessage: One change through the pointer, and x changed too. They are not two copies — they are the same memory location seen from two angles.

title: The nil pointer
instruction: A pointer that has not been assigned an address has the value `nil`. Using a nil pointer crashes your program.\nRun this. It checks for nil before using the pointer. Add a line that assigns `p = &shelfCount` and prints `*p` after the check.
expectedOutput: p is nil — no shelf assigned yet

codeChecks.message: Assign p = &shelfCount to make p point to shelfCount.
Print *p after assigning the pointer.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Always check for nil before dereferencing an unknown pointer — like checking that a shelf exists before reaching for a book.

title: Pointers and functions — the copy problem
instruction: Here is the problem pointers solve. This function tries to increase the number of book copies, but it receives a copy — so the original count never changes.\nRun it. `copies` is still 5 after the call. The function doubled the copy, not the original.
successMessage: Still 5. The function received a copy of 5, doubled the copy to 10, then threw the copy away. This is pass-by-value in action.

title: Pointers and functions — the fix
instruction: Fix the `doubleCopies` function so it actually modifies the original count.\nChange the parameter to `*int` and pass `&copies` when calling it.\nInside the function, write `*n = *n * 2` to update the value at the address.

codeChecks.message: Change the parameter to *int: func doubleCopies(n *int)
Update the value: *n = *n * 2
Pass the address: doubleCopies(&copies)
Replace the TODO comments with your fixes.

successMessage: Now it's 10. The function received the address of copies, walked to that address, and doubled the value there.

title: Pointer to a struct
instruction: When you have a pointer to a struct, Go lets you use `.` to access fields directly — no need to write `(*book).Title`. Go auto-dereferences for you.\nRun this. Then update `p.Copies` to `15` through the pointer and print it.

codeChecks.message: Update p.Copies = 15 through the pointer.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: p.Copies and (*p).Copies are identical — Go auto-dereferences for you. The dot operator on a pointer is syntactic sugar.

title: new() — another way to create a pointer
instruction: `new(T)` allocates memory for a value of type `T`, sets it to its zero value, and returns a pointer.\n`p := new(int)` — creates an int set to 0, returns a `*int`.\nRun this. Then use `new` to create a pointer to a `float64`, set it to `3.14`, and print it.

codeChecks.message: Use new(float64) to create a float64 pointer.
Set the value to 3.14 and print it.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: new() allocates and zeroes — like a reserved shelf with a blank label. Less common than &var but good to know.

title: Fix the broken function
instruction: The `returnBook` function below is supposed to set a book's checked-out status back to false. But it does not work — the book stays checked out after the call.\nFix it so that calling `returnBook` actually changes the original struct.
hint: Change the parameter to *Book and update the function body. Then pass &myBook in main.

codeChecks.message: Change the parameter to *Book: func returnBook(b *Book)
Pass the address: returnBook(&myBook)

successMessage: IsCheckedOut is now false — the book is back on the shelf. You spotted the copy problem and fixed it with a pointer.

title: Build a library catalog
instruction:
No starter code. Build a library catalog system:
1. Define a `Book` struct with `Title` and `Copies` (int)
2. Write `CheckOut(catalog *Book)` — decreases copies by 1 if available, else prints `"No copies left"`
3. Write `Return(catalog *Book)` — increases copies by 1
4. Create a book with 2 copies, check out 1, return 1, check out 2 more times, print final copies
hint: func CheckOut(c *Book) { if c.Copies > 0 { c.Copies--; fmt.Printf(...) } else { fmt.Println("No copies left") } }

codeChecks.message: Define a Book struct.
Add a Copies int field to Book.
Define a CheckOut function with *Book parameter.
Define a Return function with *Book parameter.
Replace the starter comment with your code.

successMessage: A real library catalog built with pointers. CheckOut and Return modify the original book record because they receive the address — not a copy.
